humanities_role_explanation: The benchmark utilizes literary narratives (unpublished novels from the Book Corpus) as content to construct a word prediction task. However, it treats these texts as raw linguistic data to test discourse coherence and co-reference resolution in NLP, rather than facilitating native humanist inquiry or literary analysis.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Monolingual English narrative passages consisting of a context (average 4.6 sentences) and a target sentence with the final word masked.
analytical_tasks_explanation: The benchmark evaluates the model's capacity to maintain discourse-level coherence over a broad textual window. It requires identifying tracking elements like character names (proper nouns) and prototypical event participants (e.g., inferring 'camera' from 'click the shutter') to predict a final word that is impossible to guess from the target sentence alone.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: Designed as a general language modeling and natural language understanding benchmark to test context tracking.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluated older recurrent architectures (LSTM, RNN, Memory Networks) and statistical N-Gram models active in 2016, rather than modern generative LLMs.

llm_is_primary_system.note: The primary focus is testing the discourse capabilities of neural and statistical language models.

arts_humanities_data.note: Uses narrative text from unpublished novels, mapped to Literature and Literary Theory and Language and Linguistics.

quantitative_metrics.note: Evaluates systems using Accuracy, Perplexity, and Median Rank of the target word.

published_after_march_2023.note: Published as a preprint in June 2016.

english_language.note: The paper and the dataset are entirely in English.

summary: The paper introduces the LAMBADA dataset, a word prediction benchmark consisting of over 10,000 narrative passages designed to test a model's ability to utilize broad discourse context. Passages are selected such that human evaluators can easily guess the final word when given the context, but cannot do so when exposed only to the target sentence. The work demonstrates that contemporary language models fail to capture these long-range narrative and discourse-level dependencies.
key_findings: All evaluated neural and statistical language models failed to achieve even 1% accuracy on LAMBADA, despite showing strong performance (up to 21.9% accuracy) on an unfiltered control dataset. A simple baseline of picking a random capitalized word from the passage achieved 7.3% accuracy, easily outperforming all tested models and highlighting their inability to handle broad discourse context.
